text: The surgeon performs a totally endoscopic excision of a muscle mass from the right atrium using a bug set, without robotic assistance.

A fifty-one-year-old female was admitted to the hospital because of chest pain just one day before. She had a normal medical history. Trastroxic echocardiography revealed a giant neoplasm in the right atrium.
The video shows a mass measuring 48 by 36 mm with a stalk attached to the anterior septum of the right atrium.
The complete tomography for metastatic workup was negative.
Because the tumor was mobile, moved deeply into the right ventricle during diastole that led to high risk of pulmonary embolism, so she had underwent an urgent operation to
We used the technique of totally endoscopic power sets.
The patient is positioned supine on the operating table, under general anesthesia, with double-lumen endotracheal intubation visible.
The patient's right side of the chest is elevated at a thirty-degree angle, with arms positioned along the body, preparing for surgical access.
Extended defibrillator pads are placed on the patient's chest to manage cardiomyopathy during the procedure.
The surgeon performs surgical manipulation through three trocars, each less than 1.2 centimeters in diameter, while viewing the procedure on a three-dimensional video screen.
A two-centimeter transverse incision is made in the right groin, and vasocannula are established with indirect arterial cannulation using a darkened graft HMM.

Anestomose and two sides to the right common femoral artery were not drained with bicarbonate cannulation was performed via the right femoral vein and right internal occipital vein using Senegal technique.
Three trocars are inserted into the right chest of the patient, positioned for surgical access.
A HMM trochar is positioned in the fifth intercostal space at the mid-axillary line to accommodate the three-dimensional camera for visualization during the esophagectomy.
A 12 mm trocar is placed between the anterior axillary line and mid-clavicular line in the fifth intercostal space to accommodate the main surgical instrument.
A 5mm trocar is placed in the fourth intercostal space along the anterior line for the insertion of a second surgical instrument.
Carbon dioxide is insufflated into the chest cavity, initiating controlled pneumoperitoneum for the esophagectomy procedure.
Following lung deflation, the surgeon opens the pericardium two centimeters anterior to the phrenic nerve, and a suture is placed at the pericardium junction.
The surgeon snares the superior vena cava using a pallium suture thread side tool, which is passed out through the fireman's rocker.
The inferior vena cava (IVC) is visually observed to be left free, indicating no current surgical manipulation or attachment.
Patient was in general work position.
The surgeon performs a rhiotomy, followed by four zero-pulling actions using junction sutures to expose the tumor within the surgical field.

The surgeon explores a gelatinous, reddish-brown mass with a smooth surface, approximately five centimeters in diameter. The mass is sphere-shaped and attached to the fossa obelisks, completely detached from the septum checker's pit annulus.
A nylon bag is inserted into the thoracic cavity using a trial M.M. Choker preparation, intended for the containment of the tumor habitat.
The surgeon meticulously resects the tumor from its attachment on the interatrium septum, ensuring complete removal, and places it into the nylon bag within the thoracic cavity.
The surgeon inspects the head chambers using a three-dimensional angloscope, ensuring that the tumor has been completely removed without any visible residue.
The surgeon closes the right atrium using a double-layer technique with a zero-poly running suture, ensuring secure closure of the atrial incision.

A 13-French drain is placed into the pericardial cavity, visible within the thoracic surgical field.
The surgeon uses a 12-mm trocar to extract the tumor-containing nylon bag through the trocar hole, following a reduction in tumor volume.
The surgeon inserts a 32-French drain into the pericardial cavity through a 10-mm trocar position, ensuring proper placement within the surgical field.
The total operative time and bypass time were one hundred twenty minutes and forty-five minutes respectively.
Post-operative mechanical ventilation, ten hours.
ICU time, twenty hours.
Post-operative drains in the first twenty-four hours was fifty milliliters.
